summary: I was working on Liberty Island on the morning of September 11, 2001, around 7:30 a.m., excavating a sewer pipe near the promenade between the administration and restaurant buildings. From there, we had a clear panoramic view of Lower Manhattan and the World Trade Center. It was from Liberty Island that I first heard the boom and saw the plume of black smoke coming from the World Trade Center. The island was also where I went inside to check the surveillance cameras and later watched the news coverage unfold.

The World Trade Center was a dominant feature on the Lower Manhattan skyline that I could see clearly from Liberty Island. On the morning of September 11, 2001, I witnessed a large plume of black smoke coming from the western side of Tower One after hearing a deep boom. We initially thought it was some kind of explosion or mechanical failure, but then overheard mention of an airplane, which made us realize something more serious was happening. Later, I saw a second plane flying low and directly toward the towers before it impacted, confirming the tragic event.

When I first heard about the airplane hitting the World Trade Center, my mind flashed back to a similar incident involving the Empire State Building. I recalled that in the 1950s, a military plane had flown into the Empire State Building, which was one of the few times an airplane hit a skyscraper in New York. That memory helped me process the initial shock and consider the possibility that the event at the World Trade Center might be something like that.

Living and working in New York City, I was used to hearing various noises and occasional explosions from industrial areas, but nothing like what happened on September 11, 2001. The city’s skyline, especially Lower Manhattan with the World Trade Center, was a familiar and iconic sight from Liberty Island where I worked. That day, the usual city sounds were overshadowed by the tragic events unfolding, and the presence of news helicopters and Channel 7’s coverage made the gravity of the situation very real.

While watching the events unfold on September 11, 2001, I overheard Channel 7’s announcer reporting live from the scene. Their coverage included helicopter footage showing the damage to the World Trade Center and interviews with eyewitnesses. It was clear from their broadcast that the situation was serious, and their presence helped keep us informed as we stood on Liberty Island.

ABC News was one of the main outlets covering the September 11 attacks, and I remember hearing their reporters as they described the unfolding disaster. Their broadcast included detailed accounts and footage from Lower Manhattan, helping us understand the scale of the tragedy while we were still on Liberty Island. The presence of ABC News alongside Channel 7 made the event feel very immediate and real.

The Statue of Liberty is located on Liberty Island where I was working on the morning of September 11, 2001. From the area near the statue, we had a clear view of Lower Manhattan and the World Trade Center. The statue and its surroundings were the backdrop to a day that started normally but quickly turned tragic as we witnessed the attacks from that vantage point.

Lower Manhattan was the area I was watching from Liberty Island when the attacks began on September 11, 2001. The World Trade Center, located there, was clearly visible and the site of the explosions and fires we saw. The skyline was usually beautiful and iconic, but that day it was marred by smoke and destruction, and the news coverage focused heavily on this part of the city.

On the morning of September 11, 2001, when the second plane flew low over Liberty Island, it appeared to come from the direction of Staten Island and New Jersey. The roar of the jet engines was deafening as it flew very low and fast over the canopy near us. Staten Island was part of the general area from which the plane approached, making it a geographic reference point in that moment.

New Jersey is close to Liberty Island, and on September 11, 2001, the second plane that struck the World Trade Center seemed to come from the direction of New Jersey and Staten Island. Being familiar with the area, I recognized that the plane’s low and steep approach was unusual compared to normal flight patterns from nearby airports. New Jersey’s proximity was a key part of the spatial context of the events that morning.

Newark Liberty International Airport is one of the airports near Liberty Island, and on September 11, 2001, I was aware of the usual flight patterns from there. However, the plane that flew low over us toward the World Trade Center was flying much lower and steeper than typical arrivals or departures from Newark. This made the plane’s approach stand out as unusual and alarming that day.

LaGuardia Airport is another major airport near New York City, and I was familiar with the normal flight paths from there. On September 11, 2001, the plane that flew low over Liberty Island was not following any typical approach pattern from LaGuardia or the other airports. The unusual flight path contributed to the sense that something was seriously wrong.

John F. Kennedy International Airport is one of the airports serving New York City, and on September 11, 2001, I was aware of the typical flight routes from JFK. The plane that flew low and fast over Liberty Island toward the World Trade Center was not on a normal flight path from JFK or the other nearby airports. This abnormal flight behavior was part of the unsettling experience that morning.

On the morning of September 11, 2001, while working on Liberty Island, I witnessed the initial moments of the attacks on the World Trade Center. I heard a deep boom and saw black smoke rising from Tower One, then overheard mention of an airplane having hit the building. Shortly after, I saw a second plane flying low and directly toward the towers before it crashed. The events unfolded quickly and tragically, and the news coverage from Channel 7 and ABC News helped us understand the magnitude of the attacks as they happened.
